user_request: Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.20,00,000/- paid as advance for a business transaction which failed due to Defendant's default. Plead total failure of consideration under Section 65 of Indian Contract Act. Claim refund with interest and costs. Include cause of action paragraph with accrual date, continuing nature of cause of action, valuation, court fee, and proper verification clause. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000 paid as advance for a business transaction that failed due to the Defendant's default. The suit alleges total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act and claims a refund with interest and costs.

assumptions: Jurisdiction lies with the District Court based on valuation or cause of action

7. Clause (a): Where plaint does not disclose a cause of action. Under the Code of Civil Procedure 1882, s 53, 
it was not obligatory upon the court to reject a plaint if it did not disclose a cause of action. In a Bombay decision, it 
has however been held that the court has a discretion even under the new Code of Civil Procedure. 29 Under the 
present rule, the court is bound to reject a plaint if it does not disclose a cause of act ion. A plea that there was no 
cause of action for the suit is different from the plea that the plaint does not disclose a cause of act ion. In the latter 
case, it is the duty of the court to decide the question before issuing summons. 30 The question whether the plaintiff 
had any cause of action or not was to be determined on the basis of materials (other than the plaint), which may be 
produced by the parties at appropriate stage in the suit. For the limited purpose of determining whether the suit is 
wiped out under O 7, r 11(a) or not, the averments in the plaint are only to be looked into. 31 The question is whether 
the real cause of act ion has been set out in the plaint or something purely illusionary has been stated with a view to 
get out of O 7, r 11 of the Code of Civil Procedure. Clever drafting creating illusions of cause of action are not 
permitted in law and a clear right to sue should be shown in the plaint. 32 A plaint, of

cause of act ion and liable to be rejected.63
Under the Representation of the People Act 1951, statement of material facts and particulars of alleged illegalities 
are to be furnished in an election petition. Omnibus allegations of commission of illegalities and irregularities do not 
make out a valid cause of action.64
Rejection of a plaint on the ground that it disclosed no cause of act ion is to be ordered, only on the basis of the 
allegations made in the plaint. The court cannot probe into the facts on the basis of controversy raised by the 
defendant.65
Whether the plaintiff has got legal or constitutional right or any such right has been infringed or not is an issue to be 
decided in the suit and the same cannot be considered while dealing with the plaint under O 7, r 11 of the Code. 
Rather the Court has only to see at that stage whether the plaint discloses a cause of action or not. Rejection of 
plaint at the threshold may lead to serious consequences and may defeat the ends of justice. Thus, where the plaint 
discloses legally tenable and arguable points, it should not be rejected under O 7, r 11 of the CPC.66
If on a meaningfulnot formalreading of the plaint it is manifestly found to be vexatious and meritless, in the sense of 
not disclosing a right to sue, the judge should exercise his power under O 7, r 11,Code of Civil Procedure taking

and the counsel for the plaintiff. 25 If the circumstances for invoking the courts discretion under ss 148, 149 and 151 
exist, the court can entertain the application, even though the plaintiff had the alternative remedy by way of 
appeal.26 A rejected plaint can be ordered to be restored under inherent powers of the court, 27 but this decision has 
been overruled by a Division Bench of the Kerala High Court in Mable v. Dolores 28 wherein it has been held that 
inherent powers cannot be invoked against an order rejecting plaint, when a substantive right by way of

relinquished; and 
(i) a statement of the value of the subject-matter of the suit for the purposes of jurisdiction and of court fees, 
so far as the case admits. 
2. In money suits— Where the plaintiff seeks the recovery of money, the plaint shall state the precise amount 
claimed: 
But where the plaintiff sue for mesne profits, or for an amount which will be found due to him on taking 
unsettled accounts between him and the defendant, [136][or for movables in the possession of the 
defendant, or for debts of which the value he cannot, after the exercise of reasonable diligence, estimate, the 
plaint shall state approximately the amount or value sued for]. 
3. Where the subject-matter of the suit is immovable property— Where the subject-matter of the suit is 
immovable property, the plaint shall contain a description of the property sufficient to identify it, and, in case 
such property can be identified by boundaries or numbers in a record of settlement or survey, the plaint shall 
specify such boundaries or numbers.

7. Clause (a): Where plaint does not disclose a cause of action. Under the Code of Civil Procedure 1882, s 53, 
it was not obligatory upon the court to reject a plaint if it did not disclose a cause of action. In a Bombay decision, it 
has however been held that the court has a discretion even under the new Code of Civil Procedure. 29 Under the 
present rule, the court is bound to reject a plaint if it does not disclose a cause of act ion. A plea that there was no 
cause of action for the suit is different from the plea that the plaint does not disclose a cause of act ion. In the latter 
case, it is the duty of the court to decide the question before issuing summons. 30 The question whether the plaintiff 
had any cause of action or not was to be determined on the basis of materials (other than the plaint), which may be 
produced by the parties at appropriate stage in the suit. For the limited purpose of determining whether the suit is 
wiped out under O 7, r 11(a) or not, the averments in the plaint are only to be looked into. 31 The question is whether 
the real cause of act ion has been set out in the plaint or something purely illusionary has been stated with a view to 
get out of O 7, r 11 of the Code of Civil Procedure. Clever drafting creating illusions of cause of action are not 
permitted in law and a clear right to sue should be shown in the plaint. 32 A plaint, of

cause of act ion and liable to be rejected.63
Under the Representation of the People Act 1951, statement of material facts and particulars of alleged illegalities 
are to be furnished in an election petition. Omnibus allegations of commission of illegalities and irregularities do not 
make out a valid cause of action.64
Rejection of a plaint on the ground that it disclosed no cause of act ion is to be ordered, only on the basis of the 
allegations made in the plaint. The court cannot probe into the facts on the basis of controversy raised by the 
defendant.65
Whether the plaintiff has got legal or constitutional right or any such right has been infringed or not is an issue to be 
decided in the suit and the same cannot be considered while dealing with the plaint under O 7, r 11 of the Code. 
Rather the Court has only to see at that stage whether the plaint discloses a cause of action or not. Rejection of 
plaint at the threshold may lead to serious consequences and may defeat the ends of justice. Thus, where the plaint 
discloses legally tenable and arguable points, it should not be rejected under O 7, r 11 of the CPC.66
If on a meaningfulnot formalreading of the plaint it is manifestly found to be vexatious and meritless, in the sense of 
not disclosing a right to sue, the judge should exercise his power under O 7, r 11,Code of Civil Procedure taking

cause of action and not the legal effect thereof. 13 In a construction contract, cause of act ion arises on completion 
of the work. It is not postponed till final certificate is given. Final certificate is only for purpose of payment and does 
not constitute cause of action unless the contract specifically provides to the contrary. 14

sections.body:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, and address. Use sui juris averment for competency. If any detail is missing, use the appropriate {{PLACEHOLDER}}.

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction. For territorial jurisdiction, state SPECIFIC facts: where the Defendant resides or carries on business, where payment was made, where the agreement was executed, or where the cause of action arose — with concrete place references. For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the claim amount and that it falls within this court's monetary limits.

examples.bad: The cause of action arose within the jurisdiction of this Court.
examples.good: The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which falls within the territorial jurisdiction of this Court. Further, the advance amount was transferred from the Plaintiff's account at {{BANK_NAME}}, {{BANK_BRANCH}} situated within the local limits of this Court's jurisdiction.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Cover: (1) How the parties came to know each other and the nature of their relationship, (2) What was agreed — the specific terms of the transaction/agreement, (3) What obligations each party undertook and the timeline for performance, (4) How payment was made — specific amount, mode, date, (5) What exactly constituted the defendant's failure — describe the specific default with detail, (6) What happened after the default — communications, demands, legal notice and response. Reference documents as 'Annexure [LABEL]'. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

examples.good: Despite repeated demands, the Defendant neither performed his obligations under the agreement nor returned the advance amount of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- to the Plaintiff. The Defendant's last communication was on {{DATE}}, wherein he sought further time but made no payment whatsoever.

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section in paragraph form. For each statutory provision: (1) State what the provision says — the legal rule it creates, (2) Explain what factual conditions trigger its application, (3) Show how the facts of this case satisfy those conditions. If applicable, include an alternative plea (e.g., Section 73 ICA as alternative to Section 65 ICA) using 'In the alternative and without prejudice to the above...' framing. Include defensive pleading: pre-emptively neutralize likely defense arguments (e.g., no part performance was rendered, no forfeiture clause exists, no adjustment made, no counter-claim exists). Cite ONLY provisions from the verified list.

examples.bad: Under Section 65, the Defendant is bound to restore the amount.
examples.good: Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872 provides that when an agreement is discovered to be void, or when a contract becomes void, any person who has received any advantage under such agreement or contract is bound to restore it to the person from whom he received it. In the present case, the business transaction between the parties has resulted in a total failure of consideration inasmuch as the Defendant received Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- but provided no benefit whatsoever to the Plaintiff.

must_include.description: At least one statutory section cited
Legal consequence explained (not just section named)

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose (not numbered sub-headings). Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose, anchored to the default/failure date, (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — when the cause of action further arose (e.g., failure to comply with legal notice), (3) CONTINUITY — that the cause of action is continuing because the defendant continues to wrongfully retain the money. The cause must be anchored to the failure/default date, NOT the notice date. Write in flowing paragraph form — avoid mechanical sub-headings like '(a) underlying obligation'.

examples.bad: Cause of action arose on X date.
examples.good: The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to perform his obligations under the business transaction despite being bound to do so, and the Plaintiff's right to claim refund of the advance amount thereby accrued. The cause of action further and continuously arose on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice demanding refund, failed, neglected, and refused to repay the said amount.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule (from enrichment or use placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation (must be anchored to cause_of_action_date/default_date, NOT notice_date), (4) that the suit is within time. If limitation article is not available in enrichment, use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section stating: (1) suit valuation equals the claim amount, (2) court fee calculated per applicable state court fees act. Use court_fee context from web search if available, otherwise use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section with a 3-part structure: (1) Pre-suit interest — from default date to filing date with rate and justification, (2) Pendente lite interest — during pendency of suit under Section 34 CPC with rate, (3) Future interest — post-decree until realization with rate. Justify the interest rate: state why the claimed rate is appropriate (commercial transaction, wrongful retention, prevailing bank rate, contractual understanding). Cite Section 34 of CPC for pendente lite interest.

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with at least 6 specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must state exact amounts or rate placeholders — never vague. Must include: (a) decree for principal amount with Rs. figure, (b) pre-suit interest with rate and date range, (c) pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC with rate, (d) future interest post-decree with rate until realization, (e) costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending legal notice, (f) any other relief the court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon, mapping to the annexure scheme. For each document: Serial No., Description, Annexure Label. Only list documents mentioned in evidence. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.heading: VERIFICATION
sections.body:
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

filled_sections.heading: BETWEEN
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff (Primary) is {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}; the Plaintiff is sui juris and competent to sue and be sued in law. The Defendant (Opposite) is {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
filled_sections.text:
The territorial jurisdiction of this District Court is invoked on the grounds that the Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, within the local limits of this Court’s territorial jurisdiction, and the cause of action relates to a money claim arising out of the business transaction conducted between the parties in this locality. The Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} as an advance toward the disputed business transaction and the funds were transferred from the Plaintiff’s account with {{BANK_NAME}} at {{BANK_BRANCH}} to the Defendant for performance of the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} executed at {{AGREEMENT_PLACE}}. The cause of action accrued at {{CAUSE_LOCATION}} on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} when the Defendant defaulted in performing the obligations under the said agreement and has refused to refund the amount despite service of a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and the reply dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}. The suit seeks refund of the advance with interest and costs, value Rs. 20,00,000, which is within the pecuniary limits of this Court, thereby establishing the Court’s territorial and monetary jurisdiction to adjudicate the matter. Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}.

Pecuniary jurisdiction is invoked on the basis that the Plaintiff’s claim seeks a refund of Rs. 20,00,000, which falls within the monetary limits of this District Court, and the suit is therefore maintainable in this Court for recovery of money arising from a contract and the alleged total failure of consideration.

filled_sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant first came together in a business context when representatives of both parties discussed a planned transaction at their respective offices. They subsequently formed a working relationship intended to facilitate the contemplated venture, and on or about {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} they reduced the terms to writing in a contract evidenced by Annexure A. The relationship between them at that time was that of a prospective financier and a party to be financed for a business undertaking.

2. The terms of the written agreement provided that the Plaintiff would advance Rs 20,00,000 (Rupees Twenty Lakh only) to the Defendant as consideration for the contemplated transaction, with the understanding that the amount would be treated as part payment toward the overall price and would be utilized for execution of the venture. The agreement contemplated that the Defendant would deliver the required goods or services and perform the agreed obligations by a date stated in Annexure A, namely {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}.

3. The Defendant undertook to utilize the advance strictly for the contemplated transaction and to perform the agreed obligations by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}. The Plaintiff undertook to furnish the funds on the terms set out in the agreement and as recorded in Annexure A, with the understanding that performance by the Defendant would enable completion of the venture.

4. On {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff transferred Rs 20,00,000 by bank transfer to the Defendant’s designated account. The payment was evidenced by the payment proof in Annexure B, which shows the funds debited from the Plaintiff’s account on that date.

5. By {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant failed to perform the contracted obligations and did not deliver the goods or services or refund the advance as required under the agreement. Despite the funds having been released, there remained no demonstrable performance by the Defendant by either the agreed date {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} or any subsequent time, and the Plaintiff remained unreimbursed for the advance.

6. After the default, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} to the Defendant demanding refund of Rs 20,00,000 along with interest and costs, a copy of which is annexed to Annexure C. The Defendant replied to the notice on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}, the response being annexed as Annexure D. Thereafter, the parties exchanged further communications, but no repayment or performance occurred, and the funds continued to be with the Defendant.

7. The cause of action for the suit accrued on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} because the Defendant defaulted in fulfilling the obligations and the Plaintiff remained ready to perform or accept performance, but such performance did not occur. The cause of action is continuing since then. The total value of the claim is Rs 20,00,000, and the Court fee payable is {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}. This PLAINT is filed on {{FILING_DATE}}. Verification: I, {{PLAINT_NAME}}, the Plaintiff, do hereby verify that the contents of this plaint are true and correct to the best of my knowledge and belief. Verified at {{PLACE_OF_VERIFICATION}} on {{FILING_DATE}}.

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text: Section 65 Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates a legal obligation to restore any person who has received any benefit under a contract which is void or which becomes void; in the present case, the Plaintiff Plaintiff_name paid Rs. 20,00,000 to Defendant_name as an advance for a business transaction evidenced by Annexure A, and the transaction failed due to the Defendant’s default, resulting in a total failure of consideration and leaving the Defendant in receipt of money for which no performance was rendered. The legal consequence of this doctrine is that the Defendant is bound to restore the amount to the other party, i.e., the Plaintiff, when the contract is void or becomes void or when a consideration fails, so that the Defendant cannot retain money unjustly obtained under a failed arrangement. Here, the factual backdrop satisfies the condition: the advance was paid in connection with a contract (Annexure A) and, owing to the Defendant’s default, there is a total failure of consideration; the Defendant has unlawfully retained the Rs. 20,00,000, and there is no genuine offset, set-off, or forfeiture clause that could defeat the Plaintiff’s right to restitution. Accordingly, the Plaintiff seeks a refund of Rs. 20,00,000 and claims interest on the amount from the accrual date until actual repayment, along with costs. The present suit is valued for court purposes at Rs. 20,00,000, with interest and costs to follow, and the Plaintiff asserts a continuing cause of action because the Defendant’s retention of the amount continues to enrich the Defendant unjustly until repayment is made; the cause of action accrues on the date of the failure of consideration, follows from the date when the business transaction collapsed due to the Defendant’s default, and remains actionable within the three-year limitation window under Article 47, as the accrual date will be fixed at {{PLACEHOLDER_DATE}} and a suit brought within three years of that date is timely. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if the Court were to find that the contract or a portion thereof does not render the Defendant legally obliged to restore the entire amount, the Plaintiff pleads that restitution remains the remedy under Section 65 Indian Contract Act as to the amount actually received under the void or voidable contract, and the Plaintiff accordingly seeks refund with interest and costs; the defensive arguments typically anticipated, such as no part performance being rendered, a forfeiture clause existing, adjustment or counter-claims, or any asserted pre-existing liability of the Plaintiff, are expressly negated by Annexure A (the agreement), Annexure B (payment proof), Annexure C (legal notice) and the absence of any counter-claim in the present pleadings. The Plaintiff also states that the plaint will be accompanied by a proper verification clause executed by the Plaintiff on {{PLACEHOLDER_DATE}} in accordance with the applicable rules, and the Court fee payable on the amount claimed shall be determined in the ordinary course. The cause of action for refund is not merely a claim for return of money but a restitutionary remedy grounded in the law of unjust enrichment that arises from the Defendant’s default and total failure of consideration; the Plaintiff prays for a decree directing the Defendant to refund Rs. 20,00,000 along with interest at the applicable rate and costs, and for such further or other relief as the Court may deem fit. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, the Plaintiff reserves the right to seek additional relief if found warranted by the Court’s interpretation of the contract and the facts proved at trial. The accompanying Annexures A and B establish the contract and the payment, and Annexures C and D address the notice and any response, with Annexure F containing additional supporting documents as necessary. The plaint is designed for filing in the District Court and includes a valuation for court fee of Rs. 20,00,000, a computation of interest from the accrual date, and a request for costs. The verification clause will be appended in the form prescribed by law, confirming the truth of the facts stated to the best of the Plaintiff’s knowledge and belief. The legal framework thus supports restitution of the amount paid in respect of a failed business transaction caused by the Defendant’s default, along with interest and costs, and the present pleading adheres to the statutory scheme set out in the verified statutory provisions and the annexure scheme provided with this suit. The Plaintiff thus pleads failure of consideration under Section 65 Indian Contract Act, 1872 and seeks a refund with interest and costs, while recognizing that the limitation period begins on the date of failure as per Article 47 of the Limitation Act and that the action is within the prescribed period so long as instituted within three years from the accrual date. The Plaintiff respectfully prays for relief consistent with the facts and the law set forth herein, and the Court is invited to grant restitution of Rs. 20,00,000 with interest and costs, together with such other relief as may be appropriate.

filled_sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
filled_sections.text: The cause of action for the present suit for recovery of Rs. 20,00,000/- arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to perform the obligations under the contract dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} by receiving the Plaintiff’s advance of Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} at {{PLACE_NAME}} for a business transaction and thereby caused total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act. The Plaintiff had performed by paying the full advance on the aforesaid date in reliance on the agreement, and the Defendant, by not performing the promised act by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} and ultimately defaulting on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, rendered the consideration ineffective and left nothing to perform, which constitutes total failure of consideration. The Plaintiff's right to sue for restitution thus arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. The cause of action further accrued on and after {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and continues because the Defendant continues to retain the whole amount of Rs. 20,00,000/- and refuses to refund, notwithstanding the Plaintiff’s demand; the Plaintiff served a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} demanding refund, but the Defendant did not comply and the reply dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} was not in favour, which underscores the continuing nature of the grievance. The cause of action is therefore continuing, as the Defendant's ongoing retention of Rs. 20,00,000/- constitutes a continuing breach and injury to the Plaintiff up to the date of filing this suit on {{FILING_DATE}} before the District Court. The Plaintiff values the relief at Rs. 20,00,000/- and seeks interest thereon at the applicable rate and costs; the Court Fee payable is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} and will be paid in due course. The facts pleaded establish a total failure of consideration under the Indian Contract Act, for which the Plaintiff seeks recovery of the entire advance amount along with interest and costs. Verification: I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, Plaintiff in the above matter, do hereby verify that the contents of this plaint are true to my knowledge and belief; the facts stated are correct; the relief claimed is properly sought. Verified on {{VERIFICATION_DATE}} at {{PLACE_NAME}}.

filled_sections.heading: LIMITATION
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff filed this suit before the District Court against the Defendant, invoking Article 47 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, as amended. The period prescribed for money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails is three years. The cause of action arises on the date of the failure; in this matter the date of failure is {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, which corresponds to {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant defaulted. The suit was instituted on {{FILING_DATE}} and is therefore within time since it is brought within three years of accrual.

filled_sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff values the subject matter of this suit at Rs. 20,00,000, being the amount paid as advance for the business transaction and sought to be recovered from the Defendant. This valuation is for the purposes of court fee and jurisdiction and is stated in the plaint filed before the District Court at {{DISTRICT_NAME}} on {{FILING_DATE}}. The Court Fee payable on the plaint for a money recovery suit is {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}, calculated in accordance with the applicable State Court Fees Act.

filled_sections.heading: INTEREST
filled_sections.text:
Pre-suit interest: The Plaintiff advanced Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} pursuant to the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. The Defendant defaulted by not returning the amount on or before {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, and as of the filing of this suit on {{FILING_DATE}} the sum remains outstanding. Accordingly, the Plaintiff claims simple interest on Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of 12% per annum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}. This rate is justified as a commercially reasonable reflection of the cost of funds in short-term business lending, aligns with prevailing bank lending practices for similar commercial advances, and compensates the Plaintiff for the wrongful retention and use of the funds during the period of default, in light of the total failure of consideration alleged in this suit.

Pendente lite interest: During the pendency of this suit, the Plaintiff seeks pendente lite interest on the decretal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of 12% per annum from {{FILING_DATE}} until the date of decree, under Section 34 CPC. Section 34 empowers the Court to award interest on money due on a contract at such rate as the Court may deem just, from the date of institution of suit until decree. The facts establish that the Plaintiff advanced Rs. 2,000,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} and that the Defendant continued to withhold repayment from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} onward, thereby delaying the Plaintiff’s recovery of funds and necessitating relief by way of interest during the litigation to reflect the cost of funds and the harm caused by wrongful retention.

Future interest: Post-decree, from the date of decree until realization, the Plaintiff seeks interest on the decretal amount at 12% per annum simple interest, to be calculated until full realization. The legal basis for this post-decree interest rests on the Court’s general power under Section 34 CPC to award interest on the decretal sum at a rate the Court deems just, with such period extending from decree to payment, to secure the Plaintiff’s ultimate recovery of Rs. 2,000,000 and associated costs. The rate is justified for consistency with the pre-suit and pendente lite rationale and to provide effective relief in light of the Defendant’s wrongful retention of funds.

In sum, the Plaintiff requests pre-suit interest from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} at 12% per annum, pendente lite interest from {{FILING_DATE}} to decree at 12% per annum under Section 34 CPC, and future interest from decree until realization at 12% per annum, each calculated on Rs. 2,000,000, with the rate justified by the commercial character of the transaction, the Defendant’s wrongful retention, prevailing bank rates, and the absence of any contrary contractual rate.

filled_sections.heading: PRAYER
filled_sections.text:
(a) The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, prays that the Court may be pleased to pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff against {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} for Rs. 2,000,000 being the principal amount paid by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} pursuant to the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, as a refund of the advance for a business transaction which failed due to the Defendant’s default, constituting total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. 

(b) The Plaintiff prays for pre-suit interest on Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}} per annum from {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} until {{FILING_DATE}} as compensation for the delay in return of the advance and the loss suffered by the Plaintiff, calculated as per the aforementioned rate for the stated period. 

(c) The Plaintiff prays for pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC on Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}} per annum from {{FILING_DATE}} until the date of decree on the amount claimed, being the amount due under the contract and already determined in favour of the Plaintiff. 

(d) The Plaintiff prays for future interest post-decree on the decreted amount at the rate of {{POST_DECREE_RATE}} per annum from the date of decree until realization, to be calculated and awarded as part of the final judgment. 

(e) The Plaintiff prays for costs of the suit amounting to Rs. {{COURT_COSTS}} including pre-litigation costs incurred for sending the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} amounting to Rs. {{PRE_LITIGATION_COST}} and the court fee payable on the plaint amounting to Rs. {{COURT_FEE}}, and for such other costs as the Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case. 

(f) The Plaintiff prays for such other relief, if any, as this Honourable Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case, including directions necessary to secure realization of the sum awarded. 

(g) General relief: In the alternative and without prejudice to the foregoing, grant such other and further relief as this Court may deem just and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case, in the interest of justice and equity, including any ancillary relief necessary to effectuate the aforesaid decrees and to secure full realization of the amounts awarded. 

Certified that the above prayers are consistent with the facts and law as pleaded in the accompanying sections of the plaint and are stated with the object of obtaining complete relief to the Plaintiff.

Pre-suit interest: The Plaintiff advanced Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} pursuant to the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. The Defendant defaulted by not returning the amount on or before {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, and as of the filing of this suit on {{FILING_DATE}} the sum remains outstanding. Accordingly, the Plaintiff claims simple interest on Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of 12% per annum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}. This rate is justified as a commercially reasonable reflection of the cost of funds in short-term business lending, aligns with prevailing bank lending practices for similar commercial advances, and compensates the Plaintiff for the wrongful retention and use of the funds during the period of default, in light of the total failure of consideration alleged in this suit.

Pendente lite interest: During the pendency of this suit, the Plaintiff seeks pendente lite interest on the decretal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of 12% per annum from {{FILING_DATE}} until the date of decree, under Section 34 CPC. Section 34 empowers the Court to award interest on money due on a contract at such rate as the Court may deem just, from the date of institution of suit until decree. The facts establish that the Plaintiff advanced Rs. 2,000,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} and that the Defendant continued to withhold repayment from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} onward, thereby delaying the Plaintiff’s recovery of funds and necessitating relief by way of interest during the litigation to reflect the cost of funds and the harm caused by wrongful retention.

Future interest: Post-decree, from the date of decree until realization, the Plaintiff seeks interest on the decretal amount at 12% per annum simple interest, to be calculated until full realization. The legal basis for this post-decree interest rests on the Court’s general power under Section 34 CPC to award interest on the decretal sum at a rate the Court deems just, with such period extending from decree to payment, to secure the Plaintiff’s ultimate recovery of Rs. 2,000,000 and associated costs. The rate is justified for consistency with the pre-suit and pendente lite rationale and to provide effective relief in light of the Defendant’s wrongful retention of funds.

In sum, the Plaintiff requests pre-suit interest from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} at 12% per annum, pendente lite interest from {{FILING_DATE}} to decree at 12% per annum under Section 34 CPC, and future interest from decree until realization at 12% per annum, each calculated on Rs. 2,000,000, with the rate justified by the commercial character of the transaction, the Defendant’s wrongful retention, prevailing bank rates, and the absence of any contrary contractual rate.


PRAYER

(a) The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, prays that the Court may be pleased to pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff against {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} for Rs. 2,000,000 being the principal amount paid by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} pursuant to the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, as a refund of the advance for a business transaction which failed due to the Defendant’s default, constituting total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. 

(b) The Plaintiff prays for pre-suit interest on Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}} per annum from {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} until {{FILING_DATE}} as compensation for the delay in return of the advance and the loss suffered by the Plaintiff, calculated as per the aforementioned rate for the stated period. 

(c) The Plaintiff prays for pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC on Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}} per annum from {{FILING_DATE}} until the date of decree on the amount claimed, being the amount due under the contract and already determined in favour of the Plaintiff. 

(d) The Plaintiff prays for future interest post-decree on the decreted amount at the rate of {{POST_DECREE_RATE}} per annum from the date of decree until realization, to be calculated and awarded as part of the final judgment. 

(e) The Plaintiff prays for costs of the suit amounting to Rs. {{COURT_COSTS}} including pre-litigation costs incurred for sending the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} amounting to Rs. {{PRE_LITIGATION_COST}} and the court fee payable on the plaint amounting to Rs. {{COURT_FEE}}, and for such other costs as the Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case. 

(f) The Plaintiff prays for such other relief, if any, as this Honourable Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case, including directions necessary to secure realization of the sum awarded. 

(g) General relief: In the alternative and without prejudice to the foregoing, grant such other and further relief as this Court may deem just and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case, in the interest of justice and equity, including any ancillary relief necessary to effectuate the aforesaid decrees and to secure full realization of the amounts awarded.

IN THE COURT OF THE {{COURT_NAME}}
AT {{COURT_PLACE}}

O.S. No. {{SUIT_NUMBER}} / {{SUIT_YEAR}}

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)


BETWEEN

The Plaintiff (Primary) is {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}; the Plaintiff is sui juris and competent to sue and be sued in law. The Defendant (Opposite) is {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}.


JURISDICTION

The territorial jurisdiction of this District Court is invoked on the grounds that the Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, within the local limits of this Court’s territorial jurisdiction, and the cause of action relates to a money claim arising out of the business transaction conducted between the parties in this locality. The Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} as an advance toward the disputed business transaction and the funds were transferred from the Plaintiff’s account with {{BANK_NAME}} at {{BANK_BRANCH}} to the Defendant for performance of the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} executed at {{AGREEMENT_PLACE}}. The cause of action accrued at {{CAUSE_LOCATION}} on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} when the Defendant defaulted in performing the obligations under the said agreement and has refused to refund the amount despite service of a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and the reply dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}. The suit seeks refund of the advance with interest and costs, value Rs. 20,00,000, which is within the pecuniary limits of this Court, thereby establishing the Court’s territorial and monetary jurisdiction to adjudicate the matter. Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}.

Pecuniary jurisdiction is invoked on the basis that the Plaintiff’s claim seeks a refund of Rs. 20,00,000, which falls within the monetary limits of this District Court, and the suit is therefore maintainable in this Court for recovery of money arising from a contract and the alleged total failure of consideration.


FACTS OF THE CASE

1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant first came together in a business context when representatives of both parties discussed a planned transaction at their respective offices. They subsequently formed a working relationship intended to facilitate the contemplated venture, and on or about {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} they reduced the terms to writing in a contract evidenced by Annexure A. The relationship between them at that time was that of a prospective financier and a party to be financed for a business undertaking.

2. The terms of the written agreement provided that the Plaintiff would advance Rs 20,00,000 (Rupees Twenty Lakh only) to the Defendant as consideration for the contemplated transaction, with the understanding that the amount would be treated as part payment toward the overall price and would be utilized for execution of the venture. The agreement contemplated that the Defendant would deliver the required goods or services and perform the agreed obligations by a date stated in Annexure A, namely {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}.

3. The Defendant undertook to utilize the advance strictly for the contemplated transaction and to perform the agreed obligations by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}. The Plaintiff undertook to furnish the funds on the terms set out in the agreement and as recorded in Annexure A, with the understanding that performance by the Defendant would enable completion of the venture.

4. On {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff transferred Rs 20,00,000 by bank transfer to the Defendant’s designated account. The payment was evidenced by the payment proof in Annexure B, which shows the funds debited from the Plaintiff’s account on that date.

5. By {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant failed to perform the contracted obligations and did not deliver the goods or services or refund the advance as required under the agreement. Despite the funds having been released, there remained no demonstrable performance by the Defendant by either the agreed date {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} or any subsequent time, and the Plaintiff remained unreimbursed for the advance.

6. After the default, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} to the Defendant demanding refund of Rs 20,00,000 along with interest and costs, a copy of which is annexed to Annexure C. The Defendant replied to the notice on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}, the response being annexed as Annexure D. Thereafter, the parties exchanged further communications, copies of which are collectively annexed as Annexure E, but no repayment or performance occurred, and the funds continued to be with the Defendant.

7. The cause of action for the suit accrued on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} because the Defendant defaulted in fulfilling the obligations and the Plaintiff remained ready to perform or accept performance, but such performance did not occur. The cause of action is continuing since then. The total value of the claim is Rs 20,00,000, and the Court fee payable is {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}. This PLAINT is filed on {{FILING_DATE}}. Verification: I, {{PLAINT_NAME}}, the Plaintiff, do hereby verify that the contents of this plaint are true and correct to the best of my knowledge and belief. Verified at {{PLACE_OF_VERIFICATION}} on {{FILING_DATE}}.


LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

Section 65 Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates a legal obligation to restore any person who has received any benefit under a contract which is void or which becomes void; in the present case, the Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, paid Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, as an advance for a business transaction evidenced by Annexure A, and the transaction failed due to the Defendant’s default, resulting in a total failure of consideration and leaving the Defendant in receipt of money for which no performance was rendered. The legal consequence of this doctrine is that the Defendant is bound to restore the amount to the other party, i.e., the Plaintiff, when the contract is void or becomes void or when a consideration fails, so that the Defendant cannot retain money unjustly obtained under a failed arrangement. Here, the factual backdrop satisfies the condition: the advance was paid in connection with a contract (Annexure A) and, owing to the Defendant’s default, there is a total failure of consideration; the Defendant has unlawfully retained the Rs. 20,00,000, and there is no genuine offset, set-off, or forfeiture clause that could defeat the Plaintiff’s right to restitution. Accordingly, the Plaintiff seeks a refund of Rs. 20,00,000 and claims interest on the amount from the accrual date until actual repayment, along with costs. The present suit is valued for court purposes at Rs. 20,00,000, with interest and costs to follow, and the Plaintiff asserts a continuing cause of action because the Defendant’s retention of the amount continues to enrich the Defendant unjustly until repayment is made; the cause of action accrues on the date of the failure of consideration, follows from the date when the business transaction collapsed due to the Defendant’s default, and remains actionable within the three-year limitation window under Article 47, as the accrual date is {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} (corresponding to {{DEFAULT_DATE}}), and a suit brought within three years of that date is timely. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if the Court were to find that the contract or a portion thereof does not render the Defendant legally obliged to restore the entire amount, the Plaintiff pleads that restitution remains the remedy under Section 65 Indian Contract Act as to the amount actually received under the void or voidable contract, and the Plaintiff accordingly seeks refund with interest and costs; the defensive arguments typically anticipated, such as no part performance being rendered, a forfeiture clause existing, adjustment or counter-claims, or any asserted pre-existing liability of the Plaintiff, are expressly negated by Annexure A (the agreement), Annexure B (payment proof), Annexure C (legal notice) and the absence of any counter-claim in the present pleadings. The Plaintiff also states that the plaint will be accompanied by a proper verification clause executed by the Plaintiff on {{PLACEHOLDER_DATE}} in accordance with the applicable rules, and the Court fee payable on the amount claimed shall be determined in the ordinary course. The cause of action for refund is not merely a claim for return of money but a restitutionary remedy grounded in the law of unjust enrichment that arises from the Defendant’s default and total failure of consideration; the Plaintiff prays for a decree directing the Defendant to refund Rs. 20,00,000 along with interest at the applicable rate and costs, and for such further or other relief as the Court may deem fit. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, the Plaintiff reserves the right to seek additional relief if found warranted by the Court’s interpretation of the contract and the facts proved at trial. The accompanying Annexures A and B establish the contract and the payment, and Annexures C and D address the notice and any response, with Annexure E containing further communications and Annexure F containing additional supporting documents as necessary. The plaint is designed for filing in the District Court and includes a valuation for court fee of Rs. 20,00,000, a computation of interest from the accrual date, and a request for costs. The verification clause will be appended in the form prescribed by law, confirming the truth of the facts stated to the best of the Plaintiff’s knowledge and belief. The legal framework thus supports restitution of the amount paid in respect of a failed business transaction caused by the Defendant’s default, along with interest and costs, and the present pleading adheres to the statutory scheme set out in the verified statutory provisions and the annexure scheme provided with this suit. The Plaintiff thus pleads failure of consideration under Section 65 Indian Contract Act, 1872 and seeks a refund with interest and costs, while recognizing that the limitation period begins on the date of failure as per Article 47 of the Limitation Act and that the action is within the prescribed period so long as instituted within three years from the accrual date. The Plaintiff respectfully prays for relief consistent with the facts and the law set forth herein, and the Court is invited to grant restitution of Rs. 20,00,000 with interest and costs, together with such other relief as may be appropriate.


CAUSE OF ACTION

The cause of action for the present suit for recovery of Rs. 20,00,000/- arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to perform the obligations under the contract dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} by receiving the Plaintiff’s advance of Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} at {{PLACE_NAME}} for a business transaction and thereby caused total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act. The Plaintiff had performed by paying the full advance on the aforesaid date in reliance on the agreement, and the Defendant, by not performing the promised act by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} and ultimately defaulting on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, rendered the consideration ineffective and left nothing to perform, which constitutes total failure of consideration. The Plaintiff's right to sue for restitution thus arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. The cause of action further accrued on and after {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and continues because the Defendant continues to retain the whole amount of Rs. 20,00,000/- and refuses to refund, notwithstanding the Plaintiff’s demand; the Plaintiff served a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} demanding refund, but the Defendant did not comply and the reply dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} was not in favour, which underscores the continuing nature of the grievance. The cause of action is therefore continuing, as the Defendant's ongoing retention of Rs. 20,00,000/- constitutes a continuing breach and injury to the Plaintiff up to the date of filing this suit on {{FILING_DATE}} before the District Court. The Plaintiff values the relief at Rs. 20,00,000/- and seeks interest thereon at the applicable rate and costs; the Court Fee payable is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} and the proper court fee has been paid/affixed on this plaint. The facts pleaded establish a total failure of consideration under the Indian Contract Act, for which the Plaintiff seeks recovery of the entire advance amount along with interest and costs. Verification: I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, Plaintiff in the above matter, do hereby verify that the contents of this plaint are true to my knowledge and belief; the facts stated are correct; the relief claimed is properly sought. Verified on {{VERIFICATION_DATE}} at {{PLACE_NAME}}.


LIMITATION

The Plaintiff filed this suit before the District Court against the Defendant, invoking Article 47 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, as amended. The period prescribed for money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails is three years. The cause of action arises on the date of the failure; in this matter the date of failure is {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}}, which corresponds to {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant defaulted. The suit was instituted on {{FILING_DATE}} and is therefore within time since it is brought within three years of accrual.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

The Plaintiff values the subject matter of this suit at Rs. 20,00,000, being the amount paid as advance for the business transaction and sought to be recovered from the Defendant. This valuation is for the purposes of court fee and jurisdiction and is stated in the plaint filed before the District Court at {{DISTRICT_NAME}} on {{FILING_DATE}}.

The Court Fee payable on the plaint for a money recovery suit is computed as per the applicable State Court Fees Act, on ad valorem basis, i.e., at the rate of {{COURT_FEE_RATE}} on the suit valuation of Rs. 20,00,000, amounting to Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}; the proper court fee has been paid/affixed on this plaint.


INTEREST

Pre-suit interest: The Plaintiff advanced Rs. 2,000,000 to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} pursuant to the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}. The Defendant defaulted by not returning the amount on or before {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, and as of the filing of this suit on {{FILING_DATE}} the sum remains outstanding. Accordingly, the Plaintiff claims simple interest on Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of 12% per annum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}. This rate is justified as a commercially reasonable reflection of the cost of funds in short-term business lending, aligns with prevailing bank lending practices for similar commercial advances, and compensates the Plaintiff for the wrongful retention and use of the funds during the period of default, in light of the total failure of consideration alleged in this suit.

Pendente lite interest: During the pendency of this suit, the Plaintiff seeks pendente lite interest on the decretal sum of Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of 12% per annum from {{FILING_DATE}} until the date of decree, under Section 34 CPC. Section 34 empowers the Court to award interest on money due on a contract at such rate as the Court may deem just, from the date of institution of suit until decree. The facts establish that the Plaintiff advanced Rs. 2,000,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} and that the Defendant continued to withhold repayment from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} onward, thereby delaying the Plaintiff’s recovery of funds and necessitating relief by way of interest during the litigation to reflect the cost of funds and the harm caused by wrongful retention.

Future interest: Post-decree, from the date of decree until realization, the Plaintiff seeks interest on the decretal amount at 12% per annum simple interest, to be calculated until full realization. The legal basis for this post-decree interest rests on the Court’s general power under Section 34 CPC to award interest on the decretal sum at a rate the Court deems just, with such period extending from decree to payment, to secure the Plaintiff’s ultimate recovery of Rs. 2,000,000 and associated costs. The rate is justified for consistency with the pre-suit and pendente lite rationale and to provide effective relief in light of the Defendant’s wrongful retention of funds.

In sum, the Plaintiff requests pre-suit interest from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} at 12% per annum, pendente lite interest from {{FILING_DATE}} to decree at 12% per annum under Section 34 CPC, and future interest from decree until realization at 12% per annum, each calculated on Rs. 2,000,000, with the rate justified by the commercial character of the transaction, the Defendant’s wrongful retention, prevailing bank rates, and the absence of any contrary contractual rate.


PRAYER

(a) The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, prays that the Court may be pleased to pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff against {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} for Rs. 2,000,000 being the principal amount paid by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} pursuant to the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, as a refund of the advance for a business transaction which failed due to the Defendant’s default, constituting total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. 

(b) The Plaintiff prays for pre-suit interest on Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}} per annum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} until {{FILING_DATE}} as compensation for the delay in return of the advance and the loss suffered by the Plaintiff, calculated as per the aforementioned rate for the stated period. 

(c) The Plaintiff prays for pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC on Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}} per annum from {{FILING_DATE}} until the date of decree on the amount claimed, being the amount due under the contract and already determined in favour of the Plaintiff.

placeholders_used.reason: Court fee rate is jurisdiction-specific and not provided in COURT_FEE_CONTEXT; placeholder added to show rate×value computation basis.

blocking_issues.issue: Court fee pleading is not filing-ready: the plaint states court fee "will be paid in due course" and elsewhere states a court fee amount without any rate×value computation basis in a jurisdiction where court fee is generally ad valorem for money suits.
blocking_issues.fix: Replace "will be paid in due course" with a filing-ready statement (e.g., "proper court fee ... has been paid/affixed"). In the Valuation & Court Fee section, add a rate×value computation line using placeholders (e.g., {{COURT_FEE_RATE}} × Rs.20,00,000 = Rs.{{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}) and state that the computed fee has been paid/affixed.
blocking_issues.location: CAUSE OF ACTION paragraph (court fee sentence) and VALUATION AND COURT FEE section

blocking_issues.issue: Inconsistent/invalid party-name placeholders appear in the Legal Basis section ("Plaintiff Plaintiff_name" / "Defendant_name"), risking wrong party identification if filed.
blocking_issues.fix: Replace "Plaintiff_name" and "Defendant_name" with the correct template placeholders {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}} and {{DEFENDANT_NAME}} consistently.

blocking_issues.issue: Material inconsistency in pre-suit interest period: Interest section claims from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}, but Prayer clause (b) seeks pre-suit interest from {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}.
blocking_issues.fix: Align Prayer clause (b) with the pleaded interest period (from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}}) or revise the interest pleading to match; keep one consistent accrual/start date across Interest and Prayer.

blocking_issues.issue: Document reference consistency issue: List of Documents includes Annexure E though the body does not reference Annexure E; additionally, the body references Annexure F as "additional supporting documents" without specifying what it is.
blocking_issues.fix: Either (i) reference Annexure E in the Facts/communications paragraph and clarify Annexure F in the List of Documents, or (ii) delete Annexure E (and/or F) from the List if not relied upon. Ensure every annexure in the list is referenced in the body.
blocking_issues.location: FACTS OF THE CASE (paras 1–6), LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS (annexure sentence), and LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON

blocking_issues.issue: Court fee placeholder inconsistency across sections: the prayer uses {{COURT_FEE}} while other sections use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}, creating ambiguity in the amount claimed/paid.
blocking_issues.fix: Use a single placeholder for court fee throughout (preferably {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}) and update the prayer accordingly.
blocking_issues.location: PRAYER clause (e) and elsewhere (CAUSE OF ACTION / VALUATION AND COURT FEE)

non_blocking_issues.issue: Facts section contains some conclusory/skeletal statements (e.g., "no demonstrable performance") and could be more specific about what exactly the Defendant was to do, what milestones failed, and how default was communicated.
non_blocking_issues.fix: Expand facts into a more detailed chronological narrative: specify the business transaction nature, obligations, timelines, communications, and exact breach/refusal details.

non_blocking_issues.issue: Legal analysis is mixed into the Facts section (cause of action/valuation/verification sentence embedded in Facts para 7).
non_blocking_issues.fix: Move limitation/valuation/court fee/verification statements out of Facts into their dedicated sections; keep Facts purely factual and chronological.

non_blocking_issues.issue: Cause of action paragraph is verbose and partially repetitive, and it characterizes retention as a "continuing breach" (which may be contested) without clarifying the continuing wrong vs continuing effect distinction.
non_blocking_issues.fix: Refine cause of action to clearly state origin (failure/default date), subsequent accrual (demand/notice), and continuing nature as continuing wrongful retention/non-payment (without overcharacterizing as continuing breach).

non_blocking_issues.issue: Prayer contains redundant/general relief items (clauses (f) and (g)) and includes non-pleading certification language.
non_blocking_issues.fix: Retain one standard 'other relief' clause; remove surplus certification sentence unless required by local practice.
non_blocking_issues.location: PRAYER (clauses f, g and final certification line)

non_blocking_issues.issue: List of documents numbering starts at 8 rather than 1.
non_blocking_issues.fix: Renumber List of Documents sequentially from 1.

non_blocking_issues.issue: Amount formatting is inconsistent (Rs. 20,00,000 vs Rs. 2,000,000).
non_blocking_issues.fix: Use one consistent style throughout (preferably Rs. 20,00,000/-).

non_blocking_issues.issue: Legal Basis section is a single very long paragraph, reducing readability.
non_blocking_issues.fix: Break into numbered sub-paragraphs: Section 65 basis, limitation basis (Art. 47), defensive pleas, interest basis (S.34 CPC).

unsupported_statements.statement: "there is no genuine offset, set-off, or forfeiture clause that could defeat the Plaintiff’s right to restitution"
unsupported_statements.reason: No facts are pleaded describing contract terms on forfeiture/set-off, and no document extract is provided in the draft to support this categorical assertion.

unsupported_statements.statement: "the absence of any counter-claim in the present pleadings"
unsupported_statements.reason: At plaint stage, defendant pleadings/counterclaim are not yet on record; the statement is speculative and not fact-supported.

unsupported_statements.statement: "no part performance was rendered" (as part of defensive arguments being 'expressly negated')
unsupported_statements.reason: The plaint does not plead specific acts expected/attempted and corresponding proof; it is asserted as a negation without factual particulars.

unsupported_statements.reason: A legal notice cannot prove absence of counterclaim; counterclaim is procedural and arises only upon defendant filing written statement.

JURISDICTION

The territorial jurisdiction of this District Court is invoked on the grounds that the Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, within the local limits of this Court’s territorial jurisdiction, and the cause of action relates to a money claim arising out of the business transaction conducted between the parties in this locality. The Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} as an advance toward the disputed business transaction and the funds were transferred from the Plaintiff’s account with {{BANK_NAME}} at {{BANK_BRANCH}} to the Defendant for performance of the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} executed at {{AGREEMENT_PLACE}}. The cause of action accrued at {{CAUSE_LOCATION}} on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} when the Defendant defaulted in performing the obligations under the said agreement and has refused to refund the amount despite service of a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} and the reply dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}. The suit seeks refund of the advance with interest and costs, value Rs. 20,00,000, which is within the pecuniary limits of this Court, thereby establishing the Court’s territorial and monetary jurisdiction to adjudicate the matter. Place: {{COURT_PLACE}}.

Pecuniary jurisdiction is invoked on the basis that the Plaintiff’s claim seeks a refund of Rs. 20,00,000, which falls within the monetary limits of this District Court, and the suit is therefore maintainable in this Court for recovery of money arising from a contract and the alleged total failure of consideration.


FACTS OF THE CASE

1. The Plaintiff and the Defendant first came together in a business context when representatives of both parties discussed a planned transaction at their respective offices. They subsequently formed a working relationship intended to facilitate the contemplated venture, and on or about {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} they reduced the terms to writing in a contract evidenced by Annexure A. The relationship between them at that time was that of a prospective financier and a party to be financed for a business undertaking.

2. The terms of the written agreement provided that the Plaintiff would advance Rs 20,00,000 (Rupees Twenty Lakh only) to the Defendant as consideration for the contemplated transaction, with the understanding that the amount would be treated as part payment toward the overall price and would be utilized for execution of the venture. The agreement contemplated that the Defendant would deliver the required goods or services and perform the agreed obligations by a date stated in Annexure A, namely {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}.

3. The Defendant undertook to utilize the advance strictly for the contemplated transaction and to perform the agreed obligations by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}}. The Plaintiff undertook to furnish the funds on the terms set out in the agreement and as recorded in Annexure A, with the understanding that performance by the Defendant would enable completion of the venture.

4. On {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff transferred Rs 20,00,000 by bank transfer to the Defendant’s designated account. The payment was evidenced by the payment proof in Annexure B, which shows the funds debited from the Plaintiff’s account on that date.

5. By {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant failed to perform the contracted obligations and did not deliver the goods or services or refund the advance as required under the agreement. Despite the funds having been released, there remained no demonstrable performance by the Defendant by either the agreed date {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} or any subsequent time, and the Plaintiff remained unreimbursed for the advance.

6. After the default, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} to the Defendant demanding refund of Rs 20,00,000 along with interest and costs, a copy of which is annexed to Annexure C. The Defendant replied to the notice on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}, the response being annexed as Annexure D. Thereafter, the parties exchanged further communications, copies of which are collectively annexed as Annexure E, but no repayment or performance occurred, and the funds continued to be with the Defendant.

7. The cause of action for the suit accrued on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} because the Defendant defaulted in fulfilling the obligations and the Plaintiff remained ready to perform or accept performance, but such performance did not occur. The cause of action is continuing since then. The total value of the claim is Rs 20,00,000, and the Court fee payable is {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}. This PLAINT is filed on {{FILING_DATE}}. LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS

Section 65 Indian Contract Act, 1872 creates a legal obligation to restore any person who has received any benefit under a contract which is void or which becomes void; in the present case, the Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, paid Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, as an advance for a business transaction evidenced by Annexure A, and the transaction failed due to the Defendant’s default, resulting in a total failure of consideration and leaving the Defendant in receipt of money for which no performance was rendered. The legal consequence of this doctrine is that the Defendant is bound to restore the amount to the other party, i.e., the Plaintiff, when the contract is void or becomes void or when a consideration fails, so that the Defendant cannot retain money unjustly obtained under a failed arrangement. Here, the factual backdrop satisfies the condition: the advance was paid in connection with a contract (Annexure A) and, owing to the Defendant’s default, there is a total failure of consideration; the Defendant has unlawfully retained the Rs. 20,00,000, and there is no genuine offset, set-off, or forfeiture clause that could defeat the Plaintiff’s right to restitution. Accordingly, the Plaintiff seeks a refund of Rs. 20,00,000 and claims interest on the amount from the accrual date until actual repayment, along with costs. The present suit is valued for court purposes at Rs. 20,00,000, with interest and costs to follow, and the Plaintiff asserts a continuing cause of action because the Defendant’s retention of the amount continues to enrich the Defendant unjustly until repayment is made; the cause of action accrues on the date of the failure of consideration, follows from the date when the business transaction collapsed due to the Defendant’s default, and remains actionable within the three-year limitation window under Article 47, as the accrual date is {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} (corresponding to {{DEFAULT_DATE}}), and a suit brought within three years of that date is timely. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if the Court were to find that the contract or a portion thereof does not render the Defendant legally obliged to restore the entire amount, the Plaintiff pleads that restitution remains the remedy under Section 65 Indian Contract Act as to the amount actually received under the void or voidable contract, and the Plaintiff accordingly seeks refund with interest and costs; the defensive arguments typically anticipated, such as no part performance being rendered, a forfeiture clause existing, adjustment or counter-claims, or any asserted pre-existing liability of the Plaintiff, are expressly negated by Annexure A (the agreement), Annexure B (payment proof), Annexure C (legal notice) and the absence of any counter-claim in the present pleadings. The Plaintiff also states that the plaint will be accompanied by a proper verification clause executed by the Plaintiff on {{PLACEHOLDER_DATE}} in accordance with the applicable rules, and the Court fee payable on the amount claimed shall be determined in the ordinary course. The cause of action for refund is not merely a claim for return of money but a restitutionary remedy grounded in the law of unjust enrichment that arises from the Defendant’s default and total failure of consideration; the Plaintiff prays for a decree directing the Defendant to refund Rs. 20,00,000 along with interest at the applicable rate and costs, and for such further or other relief as the Court may deem fit. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, the Plaintiff reserves the right to seek additional relief if found warranted by the Court’s interpretation of the contract and the facts proved at trial. The accompanying Annexures A and B establish the contract and the payment, and Annexures C and D address the notice and any response, with Annexure E containing further communications and Annexure F containing additional supporting documents as necessary. The plaint is designed for filing in the District Court and includes a valuation for court fee of Rs. 20,00,000, a computation of interest from the accrual date, and a request for costs. The verification clause will be appended in the form prescribed by law, confirming the truth of the facts stated to the best of the Plaintiff’s knowledge and belief. The legal framework thus supports restitution of the amount paid in respect of a failed business transaction caused by the Defendant’s default, along with interest and costs, and the present pleading adheres to the statutory scheme set out in the verified statutory provisions and the annexure scheme provided with this suit. The Plaintiff thus pleads failure of consideration under Section 65 Indian Contract Act, 1872 and seeks a refund with interest and costs, while recognizing that the limitation period begins on the date of failure as per Article 47 of the Limitation Act and that the action is within the prescribed period so long as instituted within three years from the accrual date. The Plaintiff respectfully prays for relief consistent with the facts and the law set forth herein, and the Court is invited to grant restitution of Rs. 20,00,000 with interest and costs, together with such other relief as may be appropriate.


CAUSE OF ACTION

The cause of action for the present suit for recovery of Rs. 20,00,000/- arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to perform the obligations under the contract dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} by receiving the Plaintiff’s advance of Rs. 20,00,000 on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} at {{PLACE_NAME}} for a business transaction and thereby caused total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act. The Plaintiff had performed by paying the full advance on the aforesaid date in reliance on the agreement, and the Defendant, by not performing the promised act by {{PERFORMANCE_DATE}} and ultimately defaulting on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, rendered the consideration ineffective and left nothing to perform, which constitutes total failure of consideration. The Plaintiff's right to sue for restitution thus arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}}. The cause of action further accrued on and after {{DEFAULT_DATE}} and continues because the Defendant continues to retain the whole amount of Rs. 20,00,000/- and refuses to refund, notwithstanding the Plaintiff’s demand; the Plaintiff served a legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}} demanding refund, but the Defendant did not comply and the reply dated {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}} was not in favour, which underscores the continuing nature of the grievance. The cause of action is therefore continuing, as the Defendant's ongoing retention of Rs. 20,00,000/- constitutes a continuing breach and injury to the Plaintiff up to the date of filing this suit on {{FILING_DATE}} before the District Court. The Plaintiff values the relief at Rs. 20,00,000/- and seeks interest thereon at the applicable rate and costs; the Court Fee payable is Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} and the proper court fee has been paid/affixed on this plaint. The facts pleaded establish a total failure of consideration under the Indian Contract Act, for which the Plaintiff seeks recovery of the entire advance amount along with interest and costs.
